schools.summary: Victorinus: the silence in heaven signifies the beginning of everlasting rest, but it is described as partial, because the silence being interrupted he repeats it in order (for if the silence had continued, no further course of the narrative would be required); John seeing the Lamb open the seventh and last seal, followed by a silence in heaven about the space of half an hour — the solemn hush at the opening of the last seal (John recording the opening of the final seal: 'when he had opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour'; the meaning of this strange silence, that the seventh seal opens not with a loud vision but with a pause, 'silence in heaven,' a solemn hush of about 'half an hour,' the commentators reading it variously, some with Victorinus as a foretaste of the everlasting rest of God's people, others as a pause of solemn expectation and awe before the trumpet-judgments that the seventh seal contains, the whole host of heaven still and waiting as the next great series of God's dealings is about to be unfolded, so the last seal opens upon a reverent silence, the calm before the sounding of the trumpets).

debates.question: What does the half-hour silence at the seventh seal signify?

sides.position: A foretaste of the everlasting rest of God's people
A pause of solemn expectation and awe before the trumpet-judgments the seal contains

themes: and when he had opened the seventh seal
there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour
the solemn hush at the opening of the last seal
the calm before the sounding of the trumpets

schools.summary: Gill: these are not the seven spirits of God (whose names and office differ), nor the ministers of the word (though these are often called angels in this book and blow the trumpet of the gospel), but the angelic spirits who stand before God ready to do his will; John seeing the seven angels that stand before God, to whom were given seven trumpets — the seven angels armed with trumpets to sound forth God's judgments (after the silence, John seeing the instruments of the next series of judgments made ready: 'the seven angels which stood before God; and to them were given seven trumpets'; the dignity and office of these angels, that they are the seven angels 'which stood before God,' the chief of the heavenly host standing in his immediate presence ready for his service, and 'to them were given seven trumpets,' the trumpet in Scripture being the instrument of summons, alarm, and the proclaiming of God's acts, so these seven angels are appointed and armed, each with a trumpet, to sound forth in turn the great series of judgments contained in the seventh seal).

themes: and I saw the seven angels which stood before God
and to them were given seven trumpets
the seven angels armed to sound forth God's judgments
the trumpet the instrument of summons and alarm

schools.summary: Gill: this is not a created angel, as Gabriel or any other, nor any mere man (at least not Pope Damasus, as Lyra thought), but rather Christ the great High Priest, who offers the prayers of the saints, perfumed with the much incense of his own merit and mediation, upon the golden altar; John seeing another angel stand at the altar with a golden censer, given much incense to offer with the prayers of all saints on the golden altar before the throne — the angel-priest offering the saints' prayers with much incense before God (before the trumpets sound, John seeing a scene of priestly intercession: 'another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer; and there was given unto him much incense, that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne'; the reading many give, that this angel-priest is Christ himself, or one acting in his office, presenting 'the prayers of all saints' before God, and the 'much incense' given him being the fragrance of Christ's own merit and mediation by which the prayers of the saints, in themselves imperfect, are made acceptable and sweet before the throne, so the saints' prayers ascend to God not in their own worth but perfumed with the incense of the great Intercessor).

debates.question: Who is the angel-priest offering incense with the saints' prayers?

sides.position: Christ himself, the great High Priest — the much incense being the fragrance of his merit and mediation, making the saints' imperfect prayers acceptable
An angel acting in a priestly office, presenting the saints' prayers before God

themes: another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer
that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar
the angel-priest offering the saints' prayers with incense
the incense of Christ's merit making prayers acceptable

schools.summary: Gill: the incense being put, as it was used to be, upon burning coals of fire, caused a smoke to arise like a cloud, so that the whole house or temple was filled with it; so the prayers of the saints, perfumed with the incense of Christ's merit, ascend before God; John seeing the smoke of the incense ascend with the prayers of the saints before God out of the angel's hand — the saints' prayers, perfumed and accepted, rising up before God (John seeing the prayers received: 'the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints, ascended up before God out of the angel's hand'; the picture of accepted prayer, that as the incense, kindled on the coals, sends up its fragrant smoke in a cloud, so the prayers of the saints, joined with and perfumed by the incense of Christ's merit, 'ascended up before God,' rose acceptably into his very presence, 'out of the angel's hand,' presented by the great Intercessor, so the believer's prayers, offered through Christ, do not fall to the ground but rise as sweet incense before the throne, received and answered by God).

themes: and the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints
ascended up before God out of the angel's hand
the saints' prayers ascending accepted before God
the believer's prayers, perfumed by Christ, not falling to the ground

schools.summary: Barnes: this is a new symbol, designed to furnish a new representation of future events; the same censer that offered prayer is now filled with fire and cast down in judgment; Gill notes the golden censer, whose use was to take and carry burning coals of fire; John seeing the angel fill the censer with fire of the altar and cast it to the earth, followed by voices, thunderings, lightnings, and an earthquake — the altar-fire cast down in judgment, answering the saints' prayers (the same censer that bore up the prayers now becoming an instrument of judgment: 'the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth: and there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake'; the solemn connection, that the 'fire of the altar,' the very altar from which the prayers ascended, is now cast down upon the earth, and at once follow 'voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake,' the tokens of God's coming judgment; this answering the martyrs' prayers and the prayers of all saints, God taking the fire from the altar of intercession and sending it forth as judgment on the earth, so the prayers of the saints are not only accepted but become the very ground of the judgments now to be loosed by the trumpets).

themes: and the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth
there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake
the altar-fire cast down in judgment
the fire of intercession answering the saints' prayers

schools.summary: Barnes: the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound, evidently in succession, perhaps by arranging themselves in the order in which they were to sound; Gill notes the seven trumpets were given them; John seeing the seven angels with the seven trumpets prepare themselves to sound — the angels making ready to sound the trumpets of judgment (the altar-fire being cast down, the seven angels now readying themselves for their solemn task: 'the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound'; the orderly preparation, that the angels, each holding his trumpet, set themselves in readiness, evidently to sound one after another in succession, arranging themselves in the appointed order; the solemn pause of preparation before the first trumpet, all now set for the great series of trumpet-judgments to begin, the angels poised and waiting on God's signal, so the verse marks the threshold of the trumpets, the heavenly ministers prepared and about to sound forth the judgments that answer the prayers of the saints).

themes: and the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound
the angels making ready to sound the trumpets of judgment
set in order to sound in succession
the threshold of the trumpet-judgments

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the first angel sounded and the events which followed were very dismal — there followed hail and fire mingled with blood, a terrible storm, whether to be understood of literal or figurative judgments, fearful desolations; Gill (historicist) applies it to the desolating invasions that fell upon the Roman empire; John seeing the first angel sound, and hail and fire mingled with blood cast on the earth, burning a third of the trees and all green grass — the first trumpet bringing a storm of fiery, bloody judgment on the earth (at the first angel's sounding a terrible storm falling: 'there followed hail and fire mingled with blood, and they were cast upon the earth: and the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up'; the imagery of desolating judgment, 'hail and fire mingled with blood,' a fearful tempest cast down on the earth, the effect being destruction by burning, 'the third part of trees' and 'all green grass' consumed; the interpretive variety, some with the historicist commentators reading this of the desolating invasions on the Roman empire, others of literal or figurative storms of judgment, whichever the application the first trumpet sounding forth a real and grievous judgment of God on a portion of the earth).

debates.question: What does the first trumpet's hail and fire represent?

sides.position: The desolating invasions that fell upon the Roman empire (historicist)
Literal or figurative storms of God's judgment, the application left open

themes: the first angel sounded, and there followed hail and fire mingled with blood
they were cast upon the earth
the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up
the first trumpet's desolating fiery judgment

schools.summary: Barnes: this, according to the historicist interpretation, refers to the second of the four great events that contributed to the downfall of the Roman empire; a great burning mass cast into the sea, turning a third of it to blood; John seeing the second angel sound, and a great burning mountain cast into the sea, a third of the sea becoming blood — the second trumpet bringing a fiery mass into the sea, turning it to blood (at the second angel's sounding a vast burning mass falling into the sea: 'as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea: and the third part of the sea became blood'; the figure, that 'a great mountain burning with fire,' some huge fiery mass, an image of a great destroying power or judgment, is hurled 'into the sea,' the effect being that 'the third part of the sea became blood,' a fearful token of slaughter and death; the historicist application many give, that this figures some great power or invasion falling upon the maritime parts of the empire with much bloodshed, whichever the reading the second trumpet sounding a judgment of God upon the sea, a third part of it turned to blood).

themes: and the second angel sounded, and as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea
the third part of the sea became blood
the second trumpet's burning mountain in the sea
a great destroying power falling with much bloodshed

schools.summary: Gill: by the creatures which were in the sea, having life, men are meant, the inhabitants of the Roman empire (as the Targum understands fish of mighty princes and governors); the loss of life and of ships figuring great destruction of people and of commerce; John seeing a third of the living creatures in the sea die and a third of the ships destroyed — the further effect of the second trumpet, death and ruin in the sea (the burning mountain's fall having a twofold destructive effect: 'the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died; and the third part of the ships were destroyed'; the completeness of the ruin within its bounds, that not only is a third of the sea turned to blood but 'the third part of the creatures... which had life, died,' a great destruction of living things taken by the commentators as figuring the death of multitudes of men, and 'the third part of the ships were destroyed,' the ruin of commerce, trade, and wealth that the sea carries; the historicist reading figuring the loss of life and the destruction of the maritime power and trade of the empire under some great judgment, yet as with all the trumpets the destruction limited to 'the third part,' bounded by God's hand).

themes: and the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died
and the third part of the ships were destroyed
the second trumpet's death and ruin in the sea
the destruction bounded to a third

schools.summary: Barnes: a star is a natural emblem of a prince or ruler; the falling of a great burning star from heaven indicates, according to the historicist interpretation, some important event in the downfall of the Roman empire, the fall of some leader; John seeing the third angel sound, and a great star burning like a lamp fall on a third of the rivers and the fountains of waters — the third trumpet casting a blazing star upon the springs and rivers (at the third angel's sounding a great star falling: 'there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp, and it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters'; the figure, that a 'great star,' blazing 'as it were a lamp,' falls from heaven upon the fresh waters, 'the rivers' and 'the fountains of waters,' the springs of life-giving water; the reading many give, that a 'star' figures a great person, leader, or teacher fallen from his high place, whose fall corrupts the very sources from which the people drink, whichever the application the third trumpet sending its judgment not on sea but on the fresh waters, the rivers and springs, poisoning the sources of life).

themes: and the third angel sounded, and there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp
it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters
the third trumpet's blazing star on the waters
a great leader fallen, corrupting the sources of life

schools.summary: Gill: the star is called Wormwood because of the bitter afflictions, sorrows, and distresses which it was the instrument of, just as Naomi called herself Mara because the Almighty had dealt bitterly with her; John recording that the star's name is Wormwood, that a third of the waters became wormwood, and many died of the bitter waters — the embittering of the waters by Wormwood, bringing death to many (the fallen star named and its deadly effect described: 'the name of the star is called Wormwood: and the third part of the waters became wormwood; and many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter'; the meaning of the name, that 'Wormwood,' a bitter and poisonous herb, names the star by its effect, turning the sweet waters bitter so that 'many men died of the waters' that should have given them life; the figure of bitter affliction and corrupted teaching, whether read of bitter calamities or of corrupt doctrine poisoning the springs from which souls drink, the result being death to many, so the third trumpet brings a judgment that embitters the sources of life and slays multitudes through the bitterness).

themes: and the name of the star is called Wormwood
the third part of the waters became wormwood
many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter
the sweet waters turned bitter, bringing death to many

schools.summary: Gill: some think this refers to the darkness brought upon the doctrine of Christ's person by heresy (such as confounded his two natures), the sun of righteousness obscured; the great lights of heaven smitten, a third part darkened; John seeing the fourth angel sound, and a third of the sun, moon, and stars smitten and darkened, so that a third of day and night gave no light — the fourth trumpet darkening the great lights of heaven (at the fourth angel's sounding the heavenly lights struck: 'the third part of the sun was smitten, and the third part of the moon, and the third part of the stars; so as the third part of them was darkened, and the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise'; the figure, that the 'sun,' 'moon,' and 'stars,' the great lights that rule the day and night, are 'smitten' so that a third of their light fails and a third of day and night is left in darkness; the reading the commentators give, that the darkening of the lights figures the obscuring of rulers, teachers, and the light of truth, whether by the fall of the empire's governors or by the darkening of gospel doctrine through heresy, whichever the application the fourth trumpet bringing a judgment of darkness, a partial eclipse of the light that should guide men).

themes: and the fourth angel sounded, and the third part of the sun was smitten
the third part of them was darkened
the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise
the fourth trumpet's darkening of the great lights

schools.summary: Gill: the Alexandrian copy, the Complutensian edition, and the Vulgate, Syriac, and Ethiopic versions read 'an eagle' instead of 'an angel,' which agrees with flying, and the sense is the same; the cry of woe announces the greater calamities of the trumpets still to come; John seeing an angel (or eagle) fly through heaven crying 'Woe, woe, woe' to the earth-dwellers because of the three trumpets yet to sound — the solemn threefold woe announcing the worse judgments still to come (the first four trumpets being sounded, John hearing a solemn warning of the worse to follow: 'an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound'; the division this marks, that the first four trumpets are now past and the remaining three are set apart as 'woe' trumpets of a heavier and more dreadful kind, the threefold 'Woe, woe, woe' answering to the three trumpets yet to sound, each bringing its own woe upon 'the inhabiters of the earth,' the ungodly and unrepentant, so this flying herald warns the world that the worst is yet to come, the trumpet-judgments rising to their dreadful climax in the three woes).

themes: an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe
to the inhabiters of the earth
by reason of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound
the threefold woe announcing the heavier trumpets to come
